paragraphs.body: Steel Forest: Built For No One

Before I came to Pittsburgh, I had many dreams of what the city would be like. I’ve sought through pictures on the internet, whether they were from the publicity websites or from upper-class students, and they left a deep mark in my mind—representing the second biggest city in Pennsylvania, the city of steel, and, of course, the Pittsburgh Steelers team that prevails in the audiences of American football. But there were certain questions swinging around in my mind that I couldn’t help but to continuously ponder upon: from what respect does Pittsburgh diverge from the city I come from, and what am I going to feel like living in this city?

Back then, my impression and view on American cities like Pittsburgh was somehow narrow and I had a limited imagination of what there could be: skyscrapers which stand in the center of the city (in the downtown part of Pittsburgh) and prosperous civil businesses, such as shopping malls or grand, fancy public facilities—just like what I observed in metropolitans around Asia. Originally, I was expecting to rent an apartment in the downtown area, since it seemed engaging for me to integrate into the city’s cultural atmosphere. I had a stereotype in my mind: the downtown area must be the perfect representation of this city’s culture and show the true essence of Pittsburgh. I had thought that this city, portrayed like a generous mother from my dreams, was going to embrace me with her warm, broad arms. However, I came to the understanding that often times reality is a far cry from what we anticipate. I still vividly remember the time around the third or fourth day after I arrived in the city, it was after a bus ride of roughly half an hour. The bus stopped under the PNC Bank building that almost seemed to go through the sky. It was exactly this moment that we fell into the hug of Mother Pittsburgh, and without realizing so. As we took a little walk around, the stark contrast between my original understanding and the reality that I was facing consumed me: the city seemed to merely possess numerous tall buildings, but there was nothing that felt engaging or caught attention to a normal young adult like me. There was no warmth of a hug that tied me into this new culture that I would have to spend the next nine months living in. To me, this endless view of tall buildings overpowering the streets showed the actualization of the concept “Steel Forest”. Everything was strictly built for business—what I mean by this is that those large-scale business buildings were only for businessmen and were irrelevant to normal people’s daily life. Not to mention the idea of renting apartment: there’s barely seemed to be any residential area there. From my point of view, people there were separated, in other words, alienated, from those buildings; there were only few interactions with the city itself, and consequently, I may regard people there and buildings, or the business order, as two entities. That striking difference between the people and the physical city was the point I was most surprised about. This scene was never witnessed by me within the very limiting eighteen years of my life. In my city, Fuzhou, the most flourishing region would be Dongjiekou (东街口). It shares basically the same function with downtown of Pittsburgh, but diverges from it in mainly two respects: first, as what I mentioned above, although Dongjiekou has numerous commercial skyscrapers, what differs is that it’s centered by 东方百货 (Eastern Mall), which gathers people from everywhere of this city. This mall isn’t as tall as other buildings, which may be the reason why it expresses certain intimacy to people. Second, the residential area and business area are intersected—which implies that we live right in the place where the large-scale, maybe even international, trades are taking place. All things considered, I can feel genuine humanity and life (烟火气) there.

What is the reason behind this tremendous contrast? The answer appeared in my mind almost immediately: the strategy of urban planning in two countries/cultures is different. As a result of this difference, I observed another major contrast between Fuzhou and Pittsburgh, that is, people in Pittsburgh mostly live in houses that are concentrated in one area, while in Fuzhou, super-tall apartment buildings are most common choices (this difference also resulted in residents in Fuzhou acting more indifferent than those in Pittsburgh), in order to fit in the crowded downtown area. In short, this experience challenged my stereotype on city centers to a great extent—not all countries adopt solely one planning strategy; in China, commercial area overlaps with residential area, but in the U.S. it’s the opposite, and the two areas are clearly separated.

annotations.comment: lived - I would suggest using the past tense consistently throughout the paragraph.

paragraphs.body: What I recognized as a take-away from this cross-cultural narrative was that there are multiple ways to arrange the city. Though, it is clear that what I have seen is not the only way. Otherwise, I believe all of them possess their own merits and demerits. Take Fuzhou as an example, the cost of integrate residences and business is that everyone lives in crowded places, while much more people in Pittsburgh own larger space for daily activities. Again, 万物并育而 不相害，道并行而不相悖 (Everything could prosper without harming each other, paths could be parallel without contradicting each other), cultures are beautiful due to the fact that they are plural.
